prompt: Someone writes to you: 'I hate my job but I'm scared to leave. What should I do?' Reply the way you actually would — not a generic 'it depends' answer.
response:
First, let’s call the fear what it is: it’s a perfectly rational alarm bell going off in your head. Your brain is screaming “losing income = danger,” and that primitive survival wiring doesn’t listen to pep talks about “follow your passion.” The trick is to lower that perceived danger enough that it stops vetoing every other thought you have.

So don’t imagine a dramatic leap. Instead, give yourself a 90-day “repair or exit” sprint:

1. Cut the monthly nut. Run your bank/credit-card statements and rank every expense by how miserable you’d be without it. Protect rent, transit, groceries, insurance, cell phone. Everything else gets trimmed or sold. The less you need each month, the smaller the jump becomes.

2. Build a runway. Try to bank 3–6 months of the stripped-down total you just figured out. If you’re in a real hole (credit-card debt, no cushion), it’s worth taking on freelance, weekend, or gig work—anything that goes straight to savings—not forever, just until that account is padded. This isn’t optional; a cushion is what reduces the terror.

3. Audit your misery and your leverage. Every day for two weeks jot down: what task or interaction makes you actively angry, what you’re still good at despite the hate, and who in the company (or outside) cares that you can do it. Patterns will pop up; they usually show either (a) a role adjust­ment you can negotiate that cuts the pain, or (b) a transferable skill you can sell elsewhere without starting from zero.

4. Hold two conversations. Week six: with your manager about the adjustment, and with a recruiter or friend in another industry to test marketability. Reality checks both places show whether “repair” is viable or it’s time to put exit on the calendar.

5. Pick your quit date once the cushion is solid and at least one offer (internal move or external) is in writing. Until then, you keep doing the work competently; resentment becomes tolerable once you know the end is booked on a calendar.

If any week you dip below one month of runway, you stay and refill the tank first.  
Scared means “there’s a consequence I can’t handle yet.” Dilute the consequence (the cushion), and the fear shrinks proportionally.
